coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based dual-mode sensor for highly selective detection of nitrite in food substrates through diazo coupling reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Xiangyu
Wang, Chenzhao
Wei, Shanshan
Liu, Min
Hu, Kaixin
Song, Xuewei
Sun, Guoying
Lu, Lehui
coredata.dc:description: As an antioxidant and preservative agent, nitrite (NO2 −) plays an essential role in the food industry to maintain freshness or inhibit microbial growth. However, excessive addition of NO2 − is detrimental to health, so accurate and portable detection of NO2 − is critical for food quality control. Notably, the selectivity of most carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescence sensors was not enough due to the nonspecific interaction mechanism of hydrogen bond, electrostatic interaction and inner filter effect etc. Herein, a novel fluorescence/UV–vis absorption (FL/UV–vis) dual-mode sensor was developed on basis of mC-CDs, which were prepared by simple solvothermal treatment of m-Phenylenediamine (m-PDA) and cyanidin cation (CC). The fluorescence of these mC-CDs could be selectively responded by NO2 − through the specific diazo coupling reaction between NO2 − and amino groups on the surface of mC-CDs, thus effectively improving the selectivity of NO2 − detection. The CDs-based fluorescence sensor possessed a low detection limit of 0.091 μM and 0.143 μM for FL and UV–vis methods and the excellent linear range of 0.0–60.0 μM. Furthermore, the mC-CDs sensor was employed to detect NO2 − in real samples with a recovery rate of 97.11 %–104.15 % for quantitative addition. Moreover, the smartphone-assisted fluorescence sensing platform developed could identify the subtle color changes that could not be distinguished by the naked eye, and had the advantages of fast detection speed and intelligence. More importantly, the portable solid phase sensor based on mC-CDs had been successfully applied to the specific fluorescence identification and concentration monitoring of NO2 −. Accordingly, the designed sensor provided a new strategy for the highly selective and convenient sensing of NO2 − in food substrates, and paved the way for the wide application of CDs-based nanomaterials in the detection of food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nitrite
Fluorescence
Diazo coupling reaction
Smartphone sensing platform

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (Inset: corresponding HR-TEM image), (b) size distribution, (c) AFM image and (d) Raman spectrum of mC-CDs.
(a) The FT-IR spectra of m-PDA, CC and mC-CDs, (b) XPS spectrum of mC-CDs, The high-resolution spectra of (c) C 1 s, (d) N 1 s, (e) O 1 s and (f) S 2p.

(a) UV–vis absorption, excitation and (b) emission spectra of the mC-CDs, The fluorescence stability of mC-CDs under (c) different concentrations of NaCl solutions, (d) different temperatures, (e) various pH values and (f) 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) The relative strength of mC-CDs and selectivity of NO2 − to other interfering ions (FL intensity and absorbance), (b) (d)Fluorescence spectra and absorbance spectra of mC-CDs after addition of NO2 − (0–60 μM), (c) (e) Linear relationship between F0/F and A0/A and concentration of NO2 −.

(a) FL spectra, (b) UV–vis spectra, (c) FT-IR spectra and (d) FL lifetime spectra of mC-CDs and mC-CDs-NO2 − systems, (e) The K
sv values of mC-CDs and NO2 − at different temperatures, (f) Zeta potentials and (g) CIE coordinates of mC-CDs and mC-CDs-NO2 − systems, (h) Quenching mechanism of mC-CDs.
Schematic representation of the preparation process of mC-CD and dual-mode detection and application in NO2 − sensing.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based dual-mode sensor for highly selective detection of nitrite in food substrates through diazo coupling reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun 130022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun 130022, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an antioxidant and preservative agent, nitrite (NO2 −) plays an essential role in the food industry to maintain freshness or inhibit microbial growth. However, excessive addition of NO2 − is detrimental to health, so accurate and portable detection of NO2 − is critical for food quality control. Notably, the selectivity of most carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescence sensors was not enough due to the nonspecific interaction mechanism of hydrogen bond, electrostatic interaction and inner filter effect etc. Herein, a novel fluorescence/UV–vis absorption (FL/UV–vis) dual-mode sensor was developed on basis of mC-CDs, which were prepared by simple solvothermal treatment of m-Phenylenediamine (m-PDA) and cyanidin cation (CC). The fluorescence of these mC-CDs could be selectively responded by NO2 − through the specific diazo coupling reaction between NO2 − and amino groups on the surface of mC-CDs, thus effectively improving the selectivity of NO2 − detection. The CDs-based fluorescence sensor possessed a low detection limit of 0.091 μM and 0.143 μM for FL and UV–vis methods and the excellent linear range of 0.0–60.0 μM. Furthermore, the mC-CDs sensor was employed to detect NO2 − in real samples with a recovery rate of 97.11 %–104.15 % for quantitative addition. Moreover, the smartphone-assisted fluorescence sensing platform developed could identify the subtle color changes that could not be distinguished by the naked eye, and had the advantages of fast detection speed and intelligence. More importantly, the portable solid phase sensor based on mC-CDs had been successfully applied to the specific fluorescence identification and concentration monitoring of NO2 −. Accordingly, the designed sensor provided a new strategy for the highly selective and convenient sensing of NO2 − in food substrates, and paved the way for the wide application of CDs-based nanomaterials in the detection of food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 −) is a commonly used food additive, which can not only effectively prevent the process of food oxidation (antioxidants) and degradation (preservative agents)(Kalaycıoğlu & Erim, 2019), but also inhibit the growth of food bacteria and improve the color and flavor of food, playing an essential role in food preservation(Gassara et al., 2016; Yue et al., 2018). Nevertheless, the amount of added NO2 − should rigorously be limited to avoid health problems since NO2 − can be absorbed by the human body after eating(Sudhakara et al., 2023). In addition, different treatment measures, such as bleaching, cooking, freezing, sterilization and storage time can differently affect the content of NO2 − in products(Hao, Yang, et al., 2022). Ingesting large amount of NO2 − in short periods may induce poisoning due to hypoxia, as well as increase the risk of cancer(Wang et al., 2017). According to the World Health Organization, the maximum regulated limits for NO2 − in food and drinking water(Guo et al., 2023) are 30 mg·kg−1 and 3 mg·L−1, respectively. Accordingly, it is of great significance to design sensitive, accurate and portable analysis strategy of NO2 − in food to ensure the quality and safety of food. To date, considerable efforts have been made to develop detection methods of NO2 −, including chromatography(Antczak-Chrobot et al., 2018), capillary, electrophoresis(Wang et al., 2012), electrochemistry(Haldorai et al., 2016) and chemiluminescence(Lin et al., 2011). However, these methods require expensive equipment, long periods of analysis and trained analysts, highly hindering their wide applicability. Therefore, advanced methods on the basis of the optical performances of nanomaterials have attracted increasing attention due to their numerous advantages when compared to traditional analysis routes.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of nanomaterials which have been widely concerned in the area of fluorescence detection(Himaja et al., 2015) due to their outstanding fluorescence characteristics, stable chemical and optical properties, excellent water solubility and good biocompatibility(Kaur et al., 2023). Currently, some CDs-based fluorescence nanomaterials have been explored for the identification of NO2 −. For instance, Zhao and his colleagues prepared green emission fluorescent CDs using p-phenylenediamine and chitosan as the precursors to detect NO2 − by inner filter effect (IFE) with the LOD of 1.0 μM(Xu et al., 2022). Du et al. prepared highly efficient CDs from ginkgo kernels by hydrothermal reaction(Zhang et al., 2022). These CDs had higher sensitivity for the recognition of NO2 − with LOD of 0.15 μM and linear range of 0.5–50.0 μM and the fluorescence quenching of CDs was induced by photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and dynamic quenching process. Although these NO2 − sensors had advantages in terms of simple synthesis, low cost and long emission wavelengths, the interaction mechanism of their detection system was susceptible to some factors such as the uncertainty of surface functional groups and changes in surrounding conditions, resulting in poor selectivity of the system. Considering the advantages of the special molecular recognition ability, CDs with specific functional groups were designed to meet the imperative need of highly selective, rapid and accurate analysis of NO2 − in complex food matrices(Yan et al., 2023). The interaction between NO2 − and CDs inherited the diazo coupling reaction between NO2 − and primary aromatic amine(Liu et al., 2019; Zhan et al., 2019). Based on the obvious color change of diazo coupling color reaction, the fluorescence and UV–visible absorption (FL/UV–vis) dual-mode sensor for NO2 −was developed, and the reliability of the results was further improved by the mutual correction of FL and UV–vis detection signals in different modes (Luo et al., 2023). In recent years, the use of smartphones to distinguish subtle color changes can not only effectively improve the accuracy of the sensing data but also enable the possibility of on-site and real-time detection(Yang et al., 2023; Zhan et al., 2019). Therefore, it has broad application prospects to design an FL/UV–vis dual-mode and smartphone sensing platform for high selective, accurate and rapid analysis of NO2 − in food matrices.

Herein, a novel FL/UV–vis dual-mode and smartphone sensing platform was designed for the analysis of NO2 −. As displayed in Scheme 1 , carbon dots (mC-CDs) with outstanding fluorescence performance and excellent hydrophilicity were prepared from m-Phenylenediamine (m-PDA) and cyanidin cation (CC) by simple one-step solvothermal treatment. Afterwards, the fluorescence of mC-CDs could be selectively responded by NO2 − through the diazo coupling reaction and dynamic quenching process, which produced diazo-salt and induced UV–vis absorption increase and fluorescence quenching of mC-CDs. As NO2 − concentration rose, the color of mC-CDs solution changed under both visible and ultraviolet light, achieving the detection of NO2 − by smartphone sensing platform. The signal reporters of different detection modes mutually confirmed, displaying built-in cross-reference correction for reliable detection. In addition, portable solid sensors were developed on basis of filter paper strips and hydrogel microspheres for visual analysis of NO2 − without complex instruments and operations, meeting different test requirements and exhibiting potential application value in sensing for food safety.

Sodium nitrite (NaNO2) and m-Phenylenediamine (m-PDA) were offered by Aladdin (China). Cyanidin cation (CC), citric acid (CA), histidine (His), phenylalanine (PHE), and glucose (Glu) were supplied by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water was prepared by Mingche Water Purification System Equipment (China). All materials were used without further processing or purification.

The morphology and size of mC-CDs were observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEOL-2100F). The Raman spectra were performed on a Raman spectrometer (HORIBA, LABRAM HR Evolution). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, X' Pert3 MRD) was acquired on a CALAB 250 Xi surface analysis system using Al as the excitation source. The FT-IR spectra were performed by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermofisher, Nicolet IS50) with 64 scans from 4000 cm−1 to 500 cm−1. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained on a UV–vis spectrometer (Varian, Cary50) and a fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, RF-5301PC), respectively. The aqueous solution of mC-CDs was freeze-dried on an FDU-2110 machine to obtain a yellow powder.

The mC-CDs were successfully prepared through solvothermal method(Zhan et al., 2019). In brief, 0.1 mol m-PDA and 0.05 mol CC were added in 20 mL solution (V (distilled water): V (sulfuric acid) = 9:1), sealed in a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and then heated at 200 °C for 10 h. The primary mC-CDs products were obtained after filtration (0.22 μM) and centrifugation (8000 rpm, 15 min). Subsequently, the dark brown primary solution was dialyzed with dialysis bag (MWCO = 3500) for 4 h, and the obtained solution was freeze-dried.

Initially, 0.1 mM NO2 − solution was obtained through dissolving the appropriate amount of sodium nitrite in 100 mL of ultra-pure water. Other active small molecules and interfering ions solutions were prepared in the same way. Subsequently, 100 μL mC-CDs solution was mixed to the pre-determined NO2 − solution, and the change of fluorescence intensity and absorbance of the mixture were recorded. Finally, the optical responses of various ions and small molecules such as NH4 +, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Al3+, NO3 −, F−, SO3 2−, PO4 3−, CA, Glu, PHE, L-Glu and His to mC-CDs were investigated by fluorescence and Ultraviolet spectroscopy.

The ability of mC-CDs to recognize NO2 − under strong acidic and room temperature conditions was verified by introducing different concentrations of NO2 − into mC-CDs solution. The mixed solutions were then transferred into a quartz colorimetric dish to measure the FL and UV–vis absorption intensity change of mC-CDs-NO2 − in both modes, respectively.

During the testing process on the smartphone platform, the test samples were fixed in a closed UV-light box to guarantee the stability of the light source during color taken by the mobile phone software (color picker). Unified position and angle were also selected for shooting to minimize background interference. The samples were monitored and analyzed by smartphone software under UV irradiation, and the color information (Red/R, Green/G and Blue/B) values were recorded.

The ability of mC-CDs to identify NO2 − in real food samples was evaluated. All food samples such as milk, pork, lunch meat and sausage were purchased at a local supermarket to estimate the feasibility of dual-mode sensors to detect real samples. All food samples were pre-treated on the basis of the Chinese standard method (Ultraviolet spectrophotometer method) with slight modification. The samples were broken into particles and fully mixed with 1 % (v/v) trichloroacetic acid to separate the protein and lipid. After repeated ultrasound, centrifugation and filtration, these uniform sample solutions were acquired.

The solid sensor of mC-CDs was prepared by soaking a 1 × 2 cm filter paper strip in mC-CDs solution for 30 mins and air drying. The prepared mC-CDs filter paper strips were then soaked in a series of interfering substance solutions of the same concentration for 30 min. After air drying, the solid sensors were checked under a 365 nm UV lamp, and photos were taken.

Initially, 1.0 g sodium alginate (SA) was added to distilled water of 30.0 mL, and the mixture was stirred in a water bath at 60 °C until completely dissolution. Then, the mC-CDs solution was mixed with the prepared SA solution, and mC-CDs-PSA hydrogel precursor was obtained after continuous stirring at 60 °C for 60 min. Next, the different concentrations of NO2 − (0.0–60.0 μM) were added into 2.0 mL of mC-CDs-PSA hydrogel precursor and stirred evenly. Finally, the hydrogel mixed with NO2 − solution was inhaled into a 5.0 mL syringe and dropped into 0.1 mol·L−1 CaCl2 solution at the same height to instantly form hydrogel microspheres with a diameter of about 0.3 cm (Wang, He, et al., 2023; Wang, Wang, et al., 2023; Wang, Yang, et al., 2023).

The content of the precursor m-PDA was closely related to the content of amino functional groups on the surface of mC-CDs, so it was investigated. As shown in Fig. S1, when it reached 0.1 mol, the NO2 − had the strongest fluorescence quenching effect on mC-CDs under acidic conditions. Subsequently, the mC-CDs were readily prepared via solvothermal treatment of m-PDA, CC and sulfuric acid solution, and purified by centrifugation and dialysis. The microstructure of mC-CDs was acquired through TEM and HR-TEM. The mC-CDs exhibited almost circular geometry with the average particle size of 2.38 ± 0.72 nm by counting more than 150 nanoparticles (Fig. 1a-b) (Wei, Liu, et al., 2023; Wei, Zhang, et al., 2023). In the illustration of Fig. 1a, the HR-TEM image displayed obvious lattice structure with a plane spacing of 0.21 nm, matching with the (100) plane of the graphene carbon nucleus(Wang, Wang, et al., 2023). The atomic force microscope (AFM) image illustrated that the topographic height of mC-CDs was 4.3 nm, which close to TEM analysis results (Fig. 1c). The typical Raman spectra exhibited distinct D-band and G-band absorption peaks, corresponding to sp3 hybrid carbon and sp2 carbon domains in carbon materials, respectively (Fig. 1d). Based on the relative strength of ID/IG, the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon was calculated as 0.51, indicating the sp2 domain carbonaceous core of mC-CDs have a high degree of graphitization(Du et al., 2021).

The surface functional groups of mC-CDs were investigated by FT-IR spectra. In Fig. 2a, generous hydrophilic groups were discovered on the surface of mC-CDs, such as the wide peak at 2921–3423 cm−1, indicating the presence of NH, OH and aliphatic CH groups, which endowed mC-CDs excellent water solubility. (Hao, Yang, et al., 2022). The absorption peak of m-PDA at 1606 cm−1 shifted to 1639 cm−1 in mC-CDs, suggesting the existence of polyamide structure in the mC-CDs(Wang, He, et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2023). Three peaks at 1398 cm−1, 1281 cm−1 and 873 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CN, CO and CS, respectively. The above results indicated that mC-CDs doped with N and S elements have been successfully prepared(Long et al., 2023).

More details about the surface analysis and elemental composition of mC-CDs were explored via XPS spectroscopy. The full-range XPS spectrum of mC-CDs in Fig. 2b presented four main peaks of C 1 s (284.0 eV), N 1 s (400.0 eV), O 1 s (530.0 eV) and S 2p (168.0 eV), suggesting mC-CDs were primarily composed of C, N, O and S elements(Long et al., 2023). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum appeared four peaks at 284.2 eV, 285.0 eV, 286.0 eV and 288.3 eV, which were attributed to CC/CC, CN, CO and CO, respectively(Du et al., 2021) (Fig. 2c). The presence of three nitrogen states of NH (400.5 eV), NC (401.5 eV) and NC (402.2 eV) were demonstrated by the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2d). In the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum, the peaks were linked to CO and CO at 531.6 eV and 532.7 eV(Du et al., 2021) (Fig. 2e). As illustrated in Fig. 2f, the S 2p spectrum confirmed the existence of two peaks of CSC group at 168.5 eV and 169.6 eV, corresponding to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2, respectively(Ji et al., 2021). The results of XPS and FT-IR spectra suggested that N and S elements were successfully doped into mC-CDs, and the surface of CDs could be effectively functionalized, which gave the CDs excellent fluorescence properties(Ghosh et al., 2023).

The surface composition of mC-CDs was further analyzed by 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectroscopy in D2O solvent. As displayed in Fig. S2, the peaks at 6.8–7.6 ppm were attributed to the conjugated structure generated by the benzene ring of m-PDA during the reaction process, demonstrating the presence of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms(Ru et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2023). The results suggested that mC-CDs formed aromatic species and polycyclic aromatic after dehydration, polymerization and carbonization(Wang et al., 2018).

The optical characteristics of mC-CDs were investigated through UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrometry. The mC-CDs solution exhibited yellow and bright green fluorescence under daylight and ultraviolet light, respectively (Fig. 3a). As described in Fig. 3a, the absorption peak at 272 nm was observed, which could be assigned to π-π* electron transitions of CC. In addition, a significant broad absorption peak appeared at 386 nm, primarily due to the n-π* transition of the conjugated CN bond(Li et al., 2024). The optimal excitation wavelength of the prepared mC-CDs was determined by recording the emission-related fluorescence spectra. The mC-CDs exhibited excitation-independent characteristics, which was due to the stable surface distribution state and the surface molecular state emission mechanism of mC-CDs(Wei, Zhang, et al., 2023) (Fig. 3b).

It is crucial to maintain a high stability coupled with anti-interference performance for the sensing in an excellent detection system. Therefore, the fluorescence stability of mC-CDs was measured under various conditions, including NaCl concentration, temperature, pH and photobleaching resistance. As illustrated in Figs. 3c-d and S2, no obvious precipitate was observed in the mC-CDs solution during the tests of temperature, salinity and long-term storage. And their fluorescence intensity remained almost constant, suggesting that these CDs had outstanding stability under various extreme conditions. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of mC-CDs did not change significantly in the pH range of 1.0–13.0, manifesting the ability of the sensor to analyse real samples (Fig. 3e). The fluorescence properties were almost unaffected after 100 min of 365 nm ultraviolet lamp irradiation, suggesting its strong photobleaching resistance (Fig. 3f). Under a variety of complex conditions, the fluorescence fluctuation range of the system was narrow, indicating that the mC-CDs had excellent stability for the detection of analytes in complex food samples.

Selectivity and anti-interference performance are very vital for advanced chemical sensors in the detection and analysis. Accordingly, the selectivity of mC-CDs toward NO2 − was explored samples by monitoring the fluorescence intensity of mC-CDs after the addition of interfering species commonly present in food samples, including cations (NH4 +, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Al3+), anions (HCO3 −, F−, NO3 −, SO3 2−, and PO4 3−) and amino acid biomolecules (CA, Glu, PHE, L-Glu, and His)(Wei, Zhang, et al., 2023). As described in Fig. 4a, the fluorescence intensity of mC-CDs was quenched by about 90 % in the existence of NO2 −. As expected, only NO2 − induced the fluorescence intensity of mC-CDs to decrease, and the effects of other interfering substances were negligible. Meanwhile, the absorbance of mC-CDs at 386 nm obviously increased in the presence of NO2 −, while interfering ions had almost no effect on the absorbance of mC-CDs (Fig. 4a). Subsequently, the impact of co-existing metal ions and active molecules on the absorbance increase and fluorescence intensity quenching of mC-CDs were further explored (Fig. 4a). The addition of these competing substances did not interfere with the detection of mC-CDs toward NO2 −, demonstrating the excellent anti-interference performance of mC-CDs sensor. The above experimental results suggested that mC-CDs had outstanding selectivity and anti-interference ability for the detection of NO2 − in FL and UV–vis modes.

The analysis capability of mC-CDs was investigated by optimizing the pH, reaction temperature and reaction time of mC-CDs-NO2 − system, which were critical factors in the detection of NO2 − by diazo coupling reaction(Hao, Yang, et al., 2022; Zhan et al., 2019). The fluorescence intensity of mC-CDs upon addition of NO2 − gradually decreased as the reaction time increased, and eventually reached an equilibrium within 5 min (Fig. S4). As illustrated in Fig. S5, the fluorescence response of mC-CDs-NO2 − system was almost constant in the temperature range of 20–50 °C. The variation of fluorescence relative intensity with pH was explored, and the quenching efficiency F/F0 of mC-CDs reached its maximum at pH = 2.0. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity of mC-CDs was not affected by pH, while the fluorescence quenching efficiency of mC-CDs-NO2 − system gradually increased with the decrease of pH. Under strong acid conditions, the maximum quenching efficiency of the mC-CDs-NO2 − system was achieved due to the protonation of amino groups on the surface of mC-CDs, which changed the properties and rates of the non-radiative transition process that competed with the radiative transition process.(Li et al., 2020). Thus, in order to ensure the stability of the mC-CDs-NO2 − system, the optimal conditions for detecting NO2 − by mC-CDs were determined as pH = 2.0, co-incubation for 5 min and room temperature.

The sensitivity of the mC-CDs was assessed for the analysis of NO2 − under the optimal experimental conditions. As described in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of mC-CDs decreased with the increase of NO2 − concentration. The relative fluorescence quenching intensity F0/F displayed a prominent linear relationship with NO2 − concentration (0.0–60.0 μM). The linear equation was
F 0
/ F =
0.040 ± 0.001
c
NO 2 −
+
0.796 ± 0.043
, where c represented the concentration of NO2 − (μM) (R2 = 0.9919) (Fig. 4c). Additionally, the LOD was calculated to be 91 nM on the basis of 3σ/k (k referred to the slope of the calibration curve and σ was the standard deviation of the control). Furthermore, the absorbance of mC-CDs at 386 nm gradually increased with the increase of NO2 − concentration. The A0/A displayed an excellent linear relationship with NO2 − concentration in the range of 0–60 μM, with a linear equation of
A 0
/ A =
− 0.007 ± 0.001
c
NO 2 −
+
0.995 ± 0.008
(R2 = 0.9878), and the LOD was calculated as 143 nM (Fig. 4d-e). The above results demonstrated that the detection system had excellent sensitivity for the highly selective detection of NO2 −(Hao, Yang, et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone sensing platform detection of NO2 −
ce:section.ce:para:
Interestingly, the addition of different concentrations of NO2 − (0–60 μM) to the mC-CDs solution resulted in an obviously changed color of the solution under visible light and 365 nm ultraviolet(Hao, Dong, et al., 2022). Based on this color change, the smartphone sensing platform was developed for visual monitoring of NO2 − (Figs. S7-S8). Compared with visual inspection by naked eyes, smartphone sensing platform could compensate for subtle color change that could not be visible to the naked eyes. As displayed in Fig. S7, the color deepened from yellow to dark brown as NO2 − concentration gradually rose under visible light. Moreover, the fluorescence color of mC-CDs-NO2 − system gradually changed from bright green to dark green under UV lamp, and an obvious fluorescence quenching phenomenon was observed. Afterwards, the Scheme S1 illustrated the process of detecting NO2 − by smartphone sensing platform based on the mC-CDs. The colors of the photos were converted to RGB values through smartphone APP (color picker)(Liu, Zhan, Qiu, Lin, & Guo, 2024; Wei, Zhang, et al., 2023). The linear relationships between NO2 − concentration and the (G/B) values from the photographs taken under both visible and ultraviolet light modes were then obtained. In the range of 0–60 μM, the linear equation was determined as G / B =
0.005 ± 0.0002
c
NO 2 −
+
1.865 ± 0.017
, where c was the concentration of NO2 −, while G and B represented green and blue color, respectively. The LOD was calculated as 1.48 μM. The concentration range of NO2 − (5–60 μM) exhibited an excellent linear relationship with G/B (Fig. S8). The linear equation was G / B =
0.005 ± 0.0002
c
NO 2 −
+
1.227 ± 0.012
and LOD was 1.52 μM. Moreover, the CIE coordinate of mC-CDs and mC-CDs-NO2 − system were (0.243, 0.577) and (0.292, 0.608), demonstrating that the fluorescence emission of mC-CDs shifted after the addition of NO2 − to mC-CDs, so it had the potential to be fluorescence visual sensor for food safety. Compared with the previously reported methods, mC-CDs had richer detection modes and improved the accuracy and reliability of the results (Table S1). In summary, the smartphone-assisted fluorescence sensing platform had the characteristics of fast detection speed, convenient reading and intelligence, making it have great application prospects in the convenient detection of food additives.

The quenching mechanism of sensing system for NO2 − was explored. The absorption peak of NO2 − at 209 nm did not obviously overlap the excitation and emission peaks of mC-CDs, thus ruling out Förster resonance energy transfer or inner filter effect in fluorescence quenching process of mC-CDs (Fig. S9). As described in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensity of mC-CDs gradually decreased with the increase of NO2 − concentration, while emission peak of mC-CDs redshifted from 522 nm to 530 nm, suggesting that the surface conjugated structure of mC-CDs changed significantly. Subsequently, the change was investigated by the UV–vis absorption spectrum of mC-CDs after the presence of NO2 −. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, the peak at 386 nm gradually disappeared with the addition of NO2 −, while a new peak was generated at 439 nm because the interaction between mC-CDs and NO2 − destroyed the transition process of n − π* for mC-CDs and produced new substances or conjugated structures(Zhan et al., 2019). Additionally, the infrared spectra distinctly exhibited a new absorption band of the stretching vibration of the double bonds (NN) at 2468 cm−1 after the addition of NO2 − (5 μM) (Fig. 5c). Therefore, the fluorescence response of mC-CDs mainly involved the change of conjugated structure caused by the reaction of aromatic primary amine on the mC-CDs surface with NO2 −(Tao et al., 2022; Zhan et al., 2019).

Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetimes of mC-CDs system shortened from 1.5 ns to 0.26 ns in the existence of NO2 −, demonstrating that the quenching of mC-CDs fluorescence was a dynamic quenching process (Fig. 5d). The quenching mechanism was further clarified by Stern-Volmer equation. As described in Fig. 5e, the values of K
sv were acquired from the linear equation between the F0/F of mC-CDs and the NO2 − concentration at different temperatures. With the increase of temperature, the slope of the Stern-Volmer curve increased (K
sv (0 °C) < K
sv (18 °C) < K
sv (37 °C)) (Table S2), further suggesting the occurrence of dynamic quenching via the collision process between mC-CDs and NO2 −.

The process of diazo coupling reaction between NO2 − and mC-CDs was deduced by measuring the zeta potentials. The zeta potentials of mC-CDs and mC-CDs-NO2 − systems were determined as 5.39 mV and 5.93 mV, respectively (Fig. 5f). The higher positive potential after the addition of NO2 − could be linked to the formation of a positively charged diazo salt(Wei, Zhang, et al., 2023). Due to the electron-withdrawing group of the diazo salt, the electron cloud of the non-electron lone pair did not coplane with the π electron cloud on the benzene ring, thus the n-π* transition was forbidden. Moreover, the probability of intersystem cross transition between S1 and T1 was high, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of mC-CDs. Therefore, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of mC-CDs with NO2 − was predominantly attributed to the diazo coupling reaction and dynamic quenching process (Fig. 5h).

By analyzing the content of NO2 − in a series of real samples, the applicability of the dual-mode sensor was evaluated. And the data were compared with those determined by the Chinese standard method (GB 5009.33–2016) (Ultraviolet spectrophotometer method). The GB method displayed an excellent linear relationship in the concentration range of 0–12.5 μg·mL−1 (Figs. S10-S11). The contents of NO2 − in pork, lunch meat and sausage were subsequently measured by fluorescence, UV absorption and GB method. As illustrated in Table S3, the detection results of the sensor were basically consistent with the GB method, which demonstrated that the proposed sensor was reliable for detecting NO2 − in real samples. The feasibility of mC-CDs-NO2 − system was also evaluated by adding different concentrations of NO2 − to real samples followed by measurement of the recovery rates. In Tables S4 and S5, the recovery of the quantitative peak for the detection of NO2 − ranged from 97.11 % to 104.15 % with a relative standard deviation less than 6.0 % (n = 5). Therefore, the dual-mode sensor had excellent accuracy and repeatability in real samples, and could provide a new method with outstanding optical performance for NO2 − analysis in food matrices.

Solid phase sensors were prepared in order to improve the applicability of mC-CDs. Due to small size and excellent optical and chemical stability of mC-CDs, they could be uniformly distributed in filter paper strips and three-dimensional (3D) networks of the hydrogels(Li et al., 2023). The mC-CDs solid phase sensor emitted bright green fluorescence under an ultraviolet lamp in Fig. S12-a, which could be specifically quenched by NO2 −, while other interfering ions could not display significant response. Considering that sodium alginate hydrogel had the advantages of large loading capacity and weak fluorescence background, mC-CDs fluorescent hydrogel microspheres (mC-CDs-PSA) were prepared with sodium alginate as the carrier for the visual detection and analysis of NO2 −. The fluorescence color of mC-CDs-PSA changed with the increase in NO2 − concentration in the range of 0–60 μM, and an obvious judgment could be discovered under both visible and ultraviolet light (Fig. S12-b). Compared with traditional detection methods and commercial kits, this detection systems had the advantages of low cost, fast detection speed and small influence on temperature. Overall, the proposed solid sensors had great potential for visual analysis and portable detection of NO2 − in real samples.

In summary, this work developed a satisfactory dual-mode sensor for the highly selective and accurate determination of NO2 − content in food substrates through diazo coupling reaction between NO2 − and amino groups on the surface of mC-CDs. The diazo coupling reaction resulted in significant color change under ultraviolet and visible light. Furthermore, the participation of smartphones effectively improved the color recognition ability, thus achieved quantitative and visual detection of NO2 −. The feasibility of the proposed mC-CDs was confirmed by measuring the content of NO2 − in real samples. The portable solid sensors of filter paper strips and hydrogel microspheres successfully realized the rapidly and real-time monitoring of the NO2 − concentration, providing a prospective strategy for the analysis of NO2 − in food samples.

Xiangyu Yin: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Chenzhao Wang: Methodology, Conceptualization. Shanshan Wei: Methodology, Conceptualization. Min Liu: Software, Conceptualization. Kaixin Hu: Software, Methodology. Xuewei Song: Validation, Software. Guoying Sun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Lehui Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Province Natural Science Foundation of Jilin (20220101350JC).
